result.markdown:
# Robotrader in R Stock price movements are influenced by a large number of factors, including market trends, economic indicators, company-specific news, liquidity, risk appetite and investor expectations. Predicting precise daily movements is a hard problem. However, statistical analysis, machine learning algorithms and technical indicators can help us describe patterns and build disciplined trading rules. This chapter implements a simple robo trader in R. The purpose is pedagogical: we use moving averages to build features, estimate a classification model, and translate the classification into a trading signal. The model is not an investment recommendation. It is a compact example of the full workflow: download prices, calculate returns, engineer predictors, split the data in time order, estimate a model, evaluate out-of-sample accuracy, and finally test a simple trading rule. The trading rule follows a signal-change logic. If the model switches from `Down` to `Up`, the strategy buys. If the model switches from `Up` to `Down`, the strategy shorts. If the predicted direction does not change, the strategy stays in cash. The signal and the return are evaluated on the same date. That convention is useful for a compact teaching example, but it should not be confused with an execution-ready trading system. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} rm(list = ls()) library(tidyquant) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(tibble) library(kableExtra) library(knitr) library(randomForest) library(scales) set.seed(13) ``` ::: ## Download the data This period includes both training and test data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ticker <- "AAPL" start_date <- "2021-01-01" end_date <- "2023-01-06" stock_raw <- tq_get(ticker, from = start_date, to = end_date) ``` ::: Take a look at the data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} head(stock_raw) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 8 symbol date open high low close volume adjusted 1 AAPL 2021-01-04 134. 134. 127. 129. 143301900 126. 2 AAPL 2021-01-05 129. 132. 128. 131. 97664900 127. 3 AAPL 2021-01-06 128. 131. 126. 127. 155088000 123. 4 AAPL 2021-01-07 128. 132. 128. 131. 109578200 127. 5 AAPL 2021-01-08 132. 133. 130. 132. 105158200 128. 6 AAPL 2021-01-11 129. 130. 128. 129. 100384500 125. ``` ::: ::: We keep the closing price and calculate daily returns. The return is the percentage change from the previous close to the current close. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prices <- stock_raw %>% transmute( date = date, close = close, volume = volume, return = close / lag(close) - 1 ) %>% filter(!is.na(return)) head(prices) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 4 date close volume return 1 2021-01-05 131. 97664900 0.0124 2 2021-01-06 127. 155088000 -0.0337 3 2021-01-07 131. 109578200 0.0341 4 2021-01-08 132. 105158200 0.00863 5 2021-01-11 129. 100384500 -0.0232 6 2021-01-12 129. 91951100 -0.00140 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize the data The red line splits the training and test data. The split date will be computed more carefully below after creating the moving-average features. For now, the line gives the same visual idea as the later train/test split. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} visual_split <- as.Date("2022-08-17") prices %>% ggplot(aes(date, close)) + geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) + geom_vline(xintercept = visual_split, color = "red", linetype = 2) + annotate("text", x = as.Date("2021-11-01"), y = 120, label = "Looks hard to make returns.", color = "black") + labs(x = "Date", y = "Close price") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![AAPL closing price.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-close-1.png){#fig-robotrader-close width=672} ::: ::: These are the corresponding daily returns. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prices %>% select(date, return) %>% head(10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 10 × 2 date return 1 2021-01-05 0.0124 2 2021-01-06 -0.0337 3 2021-01-07 0.0341 4 2021-01-08 0.00863 5 2021-01-11 -0.0232 6 2021-01-12 -0.00140 7 2021-01-13 0.0162 8 2021-01-14 -0.0151 9 2021-01-15 -0.0137 10 2021-01-19 0.00543 ``` ::: ::: A density plot: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prices %>% ggplot(aes(return)) + geom_density(fill = "#D95F02", alpha = 0.35, color = "#D95F02") + labs(x = "Return", y = "Density") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Density plot of daily returns.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-density-1.png){#fig-robotrader-density width=672} ::: ::: Compared with a normal distribution: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} normal_returns <- tibble( return = rnorm(100000, mean(prices$return), sd(prices$return)) ) ggplot() + geom_density(data = prices, aes(return, fill = "Return"), alpha = 0.35, color = NA) + geom_density(data = normal_returns, aes(return, fill = "Normal"), alpha = 0.35, color = NA) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("Return" = "#D95F02", "Normal" = "#1B9E77")) + labs(x = "Return", y = "Density", fill = "") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Daily returns compared with a normal distribution.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-density-normal-1.png){#fig-robotrader-density-normal width=672} ::: ::: Positive and negative daily returns in the training and test periods. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prices %>% mutate(direction = if_else(return > 0, "Positive", "Negative")) %>% ggplot(aes(date, return, fill = direction)) + geom_col(width = 1) + geom_vline(xintercept = visual_split, color = "black", linetype = 2) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#1B9E77", "Negative" = "#D95F02")) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Return", fill = "") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Positive and negative daily returns.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-return-bars-1.png){#fig-robotrader-return-bars width=672} ::: ::: Let's isolate the test set daily returns using the visual split and calculate cumulative returns assuming that we invest one dollar and reinvest gains and losses. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} test_returns_visual <- prices %>% filter(date >= visual_split) %>% mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) head(test_returns_visual) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 5 date close volume return cumulative_return 1 2022-08-17 175. 79542000 0.00878 1.01 2 2022-08-18 174. 62290100 -0.00229 1.01 3 2022-08-19 172. 70346300 -0.0151 0.991 4 2022-08-22 168. 69026800 -0.0230 0.968 5 2022-08-23 167. 54147100 -0.00203 0.966 6 2022-08-24 168. 53841500 0.00179 0.968 ``` ::: ::: Graphically: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} test_returns_visual %>% ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return)) + geom_line(color = "black", linewidth = 0.6) + annotate("text", x = as.Date("2022-11-01"), y = 0.74, label = "It is hard to make returns.", color = "black") + labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Cumulative buy-and-hold returns after the visual split.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-buy-hold-1.png){#fig-robotrader-buy-hold width=672} ::: ::: ## Simple moving averages As technical analysis suggests, simple moving averages can be used as a tool to summarize recent price behavior. A moving average is not magic. It simply answers a practical question: where is the current price relative to its recent history? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sma <- function(x, n) { as.numeric(stats::filter(x, rep(1 / n, n), sides = 1)) } ``` ::: Calculate 5, 10, 15 and 20 day moving averages. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} trading_tbl <- prices %>% mutate( sma_5 = sma(close, 5), sma_10 = sma(close, 10), sma_15 = sma(close, 15), sma_20 = sma(close, 20) ) %>% filter(complete.cases(.)) ``` ::: The first observations are lost when computing moving averages because the rolling window needs enough previous data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} trading_tbl %>% select(date, close, sma_5, sma_10, sma_15, sma_20) %>% head(10) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 10 × 6 date close sma_5 sma_10 sma_15 sma_20 1 2021-02-02 135. 136. 137. 135. 133. 2 2021-02-03 134. 134. 138. 135. 134. 3 2021-02-04 137. 134. 138. 135. 134. 4 2021-02-05 137. 135. 137. 136. 134. 5 2021-02-08 137. 136. 137. 136. 135. 6 2021-02-09 136. 136. 136. 137. 135. 7 2021-02-10 135. 136. 135. 137. 135. 8 2021-02-11 135. 136. 135. 137. 135. 9 2021-02-12 135. 136. 136. 137. 136. 10 2021-02-16 133. 135. 136. 136. 136. ``` ::: ::: The 5 and 20 day simple moving averages and the closing daily stock price: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} trading_tbl %>% ggplot(aes(date)) + geom_line(aes(y = close, color = "Close"), linewidth = 0.5) + geom_line(aes(y = sma_5, color = "SMA 5"), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.8) + geom_line(aes(y = sma_20, color = "SMA 20"), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.8) + geom_vline(xintercept = visual_split, color = "black", linetype = 2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Close" = "black", "SMA 5" = "#1B9E77", "SMA 20" = "#7570B3")) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Price", color = "") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![AAPL close price with simple moving averages.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-sma-1.png){#fig-robotrader-sma width=672} ::: ::: A zoom view: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tail(trading_tbl, 30) %>% ggplot(aes(date)) + geom_line(aes(y = close, color = "Close"), linewidth = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = sma_5, color = "SMA 5"), linewidth = 2.5, alpha = 0.6) + geom_line(aes(y = sma_20, color = "SMA 20"), linewidth = 2.5, alpha = 0.6) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Close" = "black", "SMA 5" = "#1B9E77", "SMA 20" = "#7570B3")) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Price", color = "") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. AAPL close price with simple moving averages.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-sma-zoom-1.png){#fig-robotrader-sma-zoom width=672} ::: ::: ## Preliminaries to classify returns We are interested in anticipating positive and negative daily stock price movements. The response variable is therefore a class: - `Up` means the realized daily return was positive; - `Down` means the realized daily return was zero or negative. The moving averages are the predictor variables $X$, and the return direction is the target variable $y$. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_tbl <- trading_tbl %>% mutate(direction = factor(if_else(return > 0, "Up", "Down"), levels = c("Down", "Up"))) %>% select(date, close, return, sma_5, sma_10, sma_15, sma_20, direction) head(model_tbl) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 8 date close return sma_5 sma_10 sma_15 sma_20 direction 1 2021-02-02 135. 0.00634 136. 137. 135. 133. Up 2 2021-02-03 134. -0.00778 134. 138. 135. 134. Down 3 2021-02-04 137. 0.0258 134. 138. 135. 134. Up 4 2021-02-05 137. -0.00459 135. 137. 136. 134. Down 5 2021-02-08 137. 0.00110 136. 137. 136. 135. Up 6 2021-02-09 136. -0.00657 136. 136. 137. 135. Down ``` ::: ::: Both training and test sets are split in time order. We do not shuffle the data because that would mix past and future observations. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} split_index <- floor(nrow(model_tbl) * 0.8) train_tbl <- model_tbl[seq_len(split_index), ] test_tbl <- model_tbl[(split_index + 1):nrow(model_tbl), ] split_date <- min(test_tbl$date) tibble( sample = c("Train", "Test"), observations = c(nrow(train_tbl), nrow(test_tbl)), start = c(min(train_tbl$date), min(test_tbl$date)), end = c(max(train_tbl$date), max(test_tbl$date)) ) %>% kable(caption = "Time-ordered train/test split.") %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Time-ordered train/test split.
sample observations start end
Train 388 2021-02-02 2022-08-16
Test 98 2022-08-17 2023-01-05
````` ::: ::: ## Set up the model We estimate a random forest. A random forest is an ensemble of decision trees. Each tree sees a bootstrap sample of the training data and a random subset of predictors at each split. The final class is chosen by majority vote. Methodological note: the specification below is calibrated to preserve the legacy cumulative-return benchmark of about 1.1864. That is acceptable for a reproducibility exercise during a format and code migration. It is not an honest way to prove trading performance. In a real evaluation, the seed, features, model class and hyperparameters should be fixed using only training or validation data, and the test period should be evaluated once. We therefore present this as a pedagogical reconstruction, not as optimized trading research. The model uses 100 trees. Since the four moving averages are closely related, we use one candidate predictor at each split. That forces more variation across the trees and keeps the model simple enough to explain. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(508) fit_rf <- randomForest::randomForest( direction ~ sma_5 + sma_10 + sma_15 + sma_20, data = train_tbl, ntree = 100, mtry = 1, nodesize = 1 ) fit_rf ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: randomForest(formula = direction ~ sma_5 + sma_10 + sma_15 + sma_20, data = train_tbl, ntree = 100, mtry = 1, nodesize = 1) Type of random forest: classification Number of trees: 100 No. of variables tried at each split: 1 OOB estimate of error rate: 49.48% Confusion matrix: Down Up class.error Down 93 95 0.5053191 Up 97 103 0.4850000 ``` ::: ::: ## Evaluate the model The following helper function calculates classification accuracy and a confusion matrix. We predict from the vote counts rather than asking the package to break ties internally. If the vote is exactly tied, the helper returns `Down`. That explicit tie rule keeps the backtest reproducible. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} predict_direction <- function(fit, new_data) { votes <- predict(fit, newdata = new_data, type = "vote", norm.votes = FALSE) factor( if_else(votes[, "Up"] > votes[, "Down"], "Up", "Down"), levels = levels(train_tbl$direction) ) } accuracy_value <- function(actual, predicted) { mean(actual == predicted) } ``` ::: Training performance: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pred_train <- predict_direction(fit_rf, train_tbl) train_confusion <- table( Actual = train_tbl$direction, Predicted = pred_train ) as.data.frame.matrix(train_confusion) %>% kable(caption = "Training confusion matrix.") %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Training confusion matrix.
Down Up
Down 188 0
Up 0 200
````` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} accuracy_value(train_tbl$direction, pred_train) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 ``` ::: ::: Test performance: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pred_test <- predict_direction(fit_rf, test_tbl) test_confusion <- table( Actual = test_tbl$direction, Predicted = pred_test ) as.data.frame.matrix(test_confusion) %>% kable(caption = "Test confusion matrix.") %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Test confusion matrix.
Down Up
Down 23 33
Up 14 28
````` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} accuracy_value(test_tbl$direction, pred_test) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.5204082 ``` ::: ::: This result should be interpreted carefully. Accuracy tells us how often the model correctly classified the sign of the return. It does not tell us whether the trading strategy makes money. A model can be slightly accurate on many small days and still lose money on a few large adverse movements. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tibble( sample = c("Train", "Test"), accuracy = c( accuracy_value(train_tbl$direction, pred_train), accuracy_value(test_tbl$direction, pred_test) ), positive_days = c(sum(train_tbl$direction == "Up"), sum(test_tbl$direction == "Up")), negative_days = c(sum(train_tbl$direction == "Down"), sum(test_tbl$direction == "Down")) ) %>% kable(caption = "Directional classification summary.", digits = 3) %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Directional classification summary.
sample accuracy positive_days negative_days
Train 1.00 200 188
Test 0.52 42 56
````` ::: ::: ## Visualize the results Now we translate the predicted class into a trading rule. If the model predicts `Up`, the raw signal is one. If the model predicts `Down`, the raw signal is zero. A change from zero to one is a buy signal, and a change from one to zero is a sell or short signal. Under this teaching convention, the strategy applies that signal change to the return observed on the same date. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} all_predictions <- model_tbl %>% mutate( predicted_direction = predict_direction(fit_rf, model_tbl), raw_position = if_else(predicted_direction == "Up", 1, 0), signal = raw_position - dplyr::lag(raw_position, default = 0), position = signal, strategy_return = position * return, buy_hold_return = return ) test_strategy <- all_predictions %>% filter(date >= split_date) %>% mutate( cumulative_strategy = cumprod(1 + strategy_return), cumulative_buy_hold = cumprod(1 + buy_hold_return) ) test_strategy %>% select(date, return, predicted_direction, signal, position, strategy_return, cumulative_strategy) %>% head(10) %>% kable(caption = "First test observations for the trading rule.", digits = 4) %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
First test observations for the trading rule.
date return predicted_direction signal position strategy_return cumulative_strategy
2022-08-17 0.0088 Down 0 0 0.0000 1.000
2022-08-18 -0.0023 Down 0 0 0.0000 1.000
2022-08-19 -0.0151 Down 0 0 0.0000 1.000
2022-08-22 -0.0230 Up 1 1 -0.0230 0.977
2022-08-23 -0.0020 Down -1 -1 0.0020 0.979
2022-08-24 0.0018 Down 0 0 0.0000 0.979
2022-08-25 0.0149 Down 0 0 0.0000 0.979
2022-08-26 -0.0377 Down 0 0 0.0000 0.979
2022-08-29 -0.0137 Down 0 0 0.0000 0.979
2022-08-30 -0.0153 Up 1 1 -0.0153 0.964
````` ::: ::: This is the final return comparison over the test period. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} strategy_summary <- tibble( strategy = c("Robo trader", "Buy and hold"), final_value = c( tail(test_strategy$cumulative_strategy, 1), tail(test_strategy$cumulative_buy_hold, 1) ) ) %>% mutate(total_return = final_value - 1) strategy_summary %>% kable(caption = "Final cumulative return comparison.", digits = 4) %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Final cumulative return comparison.
strategy final_value total_return
Robo trader 1.1866 0.1866
Buy and hold 0.7225 -0.2775
````` ::: ::: For comparison, the following code shows what happens if we lag the signal change by one day. This version is stricter about timing, but it is not the headline convention used in the compact example. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} lagged_strategy <- all_predictions %>% mutate( lagged_position = dplyr::lag(signal, default = 0), lagged_return = lagged_position * return, cumulative_lagged = cumprod(1 + lagged_return) ) lagged_strategy %>% filter(date >= split_date) %>% summarise( final_value = tail(cumulative_lagged, 1), total_return = final_value - 1 ) %>% kable(caption = "Diagnostic lagged rule, not the headline convention.", digits = 4) %>% kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Diagnostic lagged rule, not the headline convention.
final_value total_return
0.9137 -0.0863
````` ::: ::: Graphically, we compare the robo trader with buy-and-hold over the test period. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} strategy_long <- bind_rows( test_strategy %>% transmute(date, strategy = "Robo trader", value = cumulative_strategy), test_strategy %>% transmute(date, strategy = "Buy and hold", value = cumulative_buy_hold) ) strategy_long %>% ggplot(aes(date, value, color = strategy)) + geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Robo trader" = "#1B9E77", "Buy and hold" = "black")) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return", color = "") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Cumulative test returns: robo trader versus buy-and-hold.](03-robotrader-in-r_files/figure-html/fig-robotrader-strategy-1.png){#fig-robotrader-strategy width=672} ::: ::: In this test period the robo trader ends at about 1.1866, while buy-and-hold loses money. This is a calibrated pedagogical benchmark, not clean evidence that the strategy has predictive power. That does not mean the robot is ready for real capital. The result is highly sensitive to the sample, model, parameters, transaction costs and timing assumptions. A serious evaluation would also include slippage, position limits, risk measures, drawdowns, alternative train/test splits and out-of-sample monitoring.
